summary.concise_summary: The team reviewed progress on LinkedIn URL normalization for the lead scraping pipeline, where a new function strips query parameters to produce clean company URLs. The main discussion focused on defining a cost-effective enrichment strategy using Apollo's verification flags to filter and rank decision-makers by role relevance before fetching full contact details. The team agreed on a human-in-the-loop workflow using a Streamlit UI or Google Sheet for manual approval of leads and email drafts before outreach. Next steps include validating Apollo vs Lusha across 20–25 companies, building the review UI, implementing hyper-personalized email generation, and investigating SMTP bounce tracking.
key_discussion_points: LinkedIn URL normalization: stripping query parameters (e.g., ?trk=...) from scraped URLs to produce clean, unique company identifiers
Using LinkedIn company ID instead of company name as the primary enrichment API input to avoid name-uniqueness issues
Cost-effective enrichment strategy: filtering Apollo results with email_status and has_phone flags before fetching full contact records
Decision-maker identification: ranking candidates by role relevance using an LLM, with web search to validate real-world availability
Human-in-the-loop UI: Streamlit app or Google Sheet for daily lead review and email draft approval before sending
Hyper-personalized email generation and multi-touch follow-up cadence
SMTP bounce handling and email delivery tracking feasibility
Architecture: building as individual functions first, then integrating into an agentic workflow
decisions_made: Use the LinkedIn company ID (not company name) as the primary lookup key for enrichment APIs to ensure uniqueness
Filter Apollo enrichment results using email_status (verified) and has_phone flags before fetching full contact details to conserve API credits
Rank filtered contacts by role relevance and fetch full details only for the top 1–3 highest-confidence contacts
Skip cross-API email verification for now; accept the risk to move forward without dual-source validation
Build a Streamlit UI (or Google Sheet as fallback) for human review and approval of leads and email drafts
Build V1 focused on scraping, enrichment, and the review UI; defer email tracking and bounce handling to V2
open_questions: Which enrichment API performs better for this use case: Apollo or Lusha?
Is it feasible to programmatically track SMTP bounce / undelivered responses from the mail server?
How should email open and click tracking be implemented via deep links?
What is the exact role-relevance ranking logic for identifying the right decision-maker across different job categories?
How should the multi-touch follow-up email cadence be structured (timing, content evolution, call-to-action)?
What are the specific target industries, geographies, and role parameters for the outreach campaign?
missing_information: No specific deadline was agreed upon for completing the enrichment validation and UI
The content strategy referenced by Dinuke has not been defined or shared
The exact role hierarchy logic for decision-maker selection (e.g., CTO vs Head of AI vs Head of Technology) was discussed but not formally decided
The Lusha API capabilities and pricing were not reviewed in this meeting

action_items.action: Add web search capability to decision-maker ranking pipeline and integrate results with the LLM-based ranking logic
Build enrichment validation: filter Apollo results by email_status and has_phone, rank by role relevance, fetch top 3 contacts, and document results for 20–25 companies
Build Streamlit UI with sections for pre-configuration, daily leads table, human approval workflow, email send trigger, status/history tracking, and hyper-personalized email preview
Define target roles and outreach parameters; coordinate with Ilham on Streamlit UI design and workflow
Investigate SMTP bounce and delivery failure tracking feasibility and report findings to Dinuke
Compare Apollo vs Lusha enrichment APIs on 20–25 test companies and document results to support provider selection decision
Support Ilham on enrichment validation and Streamlit UI development
Collaborate with Heshan and Naufa on content strategy to improve email response rates and define multi-touch follow-up cadence

flagged_issues: All action items are missing specific due dates — target completion was described as 'next week' but no calendar date was confirmed
Action: 'Collaborate on content strategy' is vague — the specific deliverable and owner split between Ilham, Heshan, and Naufa was not clarified
Action: 'Add web search to decision-maker ranking' — it is unclear whether Ilham should implement this independently or wait for Mazar's instructions

summary.concise_summary: The team addressed two main workstreams. On the property valuation model, they agreed to use an allocation method — roughly 20% of average neighborhood sale price, adjusted for lot size — to estimate land values for Lancaster, and resolved to exclude improvement values from the comparison to create an apples-to-apples evaluation against Ernie's tool, which also lacks improvement data. Mazar committed to incorporating the formula and delivering updated model output before the following Wednesday's SAM call, with Ryan planning to manually appraise a small set of properties for comparison. On the VoiceFlow chatbot, the team reviewed branding customization options and agreed to upgrade to the $60/month Pro subscription to enable white-label presentation for an upcoming demo, brand the chatbot as 'Ava' using PowerAppeal's color scheme and logo, and deploy it as a JavaScript widget on the PowerAppeal staging site. The team outlined a per-jurisdiction architecture using separate VoiceFlow projects with dedicated knowledge bases, and noted a future path to integrate live CAMA data via the PowerAppeal API and a Cloudflare tunnel. A brief image labeling update confirmed approximately 100 verified samples toward a target of 150 training and 50 test samples, and a support meeting with Andrea was proposed to address her challenges.
key_discussion_points: Land value estimation using allocation method: approximately 20% of the average neighborhood sale price, adjusted proportionally for lot size differences
Excluding improvement and cost-side values from the Lancaster model comparison to achieve apples-to-apples evaluation, since Ernie's tool also lacks improvement data

User identity verification and rate-limiting via user ID to protect against credit abuse

Brand the chatbot as 'Ava' using PowerAppeal's existing color scheme and the A-with-hash-lines logo

Deploy the VoiceFlow chatbot as a JavaScript widget on the PowerAppeal staging site before moving to production

summary.concise_summary: The team aligned on four architectural and UX decisions for the Metis platform. First, overlapping work experiences in the Career Trajectory UI will display the longest-duration role as primary (left-aligned) with concurrent roles offset to the right, with a dynamic timeline highlight in blue when a role card is clicked. Second, Total Years of Experience will be calculated as the span from the first job's start date to the last job's end date minus breaks longer than six months, while Industry Experience is calculated independently by summing time spent in each industry. Third, company industry and description will be sourced via AI (Grok) instead of unreliable LinkedIn scraping, using only the company name as input. Fourth, login and authentication will be implemented immediately to support a multi-client SaaS model with shared core data and client-specific AI summaries, with initial test accounts for Celestial and HNC. A new pre-sprint wireframe review process with Lulu and Oshady was also established to reduce rework.
key_discussion_points: Career Trajectory UI: the longest-duration overlapping role is displayed as primary (left-aligned); shorter concurrent roles are indented to the right to signal concurrency
Dynamic timeline: clicking a role card highlights its specific duration in blue on the timeline
Ordering logic: current roles ordered by total years at the company; past roles ordered chronologically by start date
Bug fix required: missing from-to dates for current roles in the existing UI
Total Years of Experience: span from first job start date to last job end date (or present), with breaks longer than 6 months deducted; breaks under 6 months ignored
Industry Experience: calculated separately by summing duration per industry across all jobs; does not necessarily equal Total YOE
UI feedback: unlink the auto-update between Total and Industry Experience fields; start all industry experience rings from the 12 o'clock position
LinkedIn unreliable for company industry and information, especially for senior profiles and startups
New data source: use AI (Grok) for company enrichment — scrape only company name from LinkedIn, then use Grok to derive industry and company summary
Candidate profile header title = title from the primary role (longest-duration role on the career trajectory)
Authentication must be implemented now to enable a multi-client SaaS architecture; deferring it would require a costly data structure refactor
Data architecture: Core Data (raw LinkedIn profiles, shared across clients) vs. Summarized Data (AI-generated summaries, client-specific)
Initial SaaS test accounts to be created for Celestial and HNC
New pre-sprint process: dedicated wireframe Q&A session with Lulu and Oshady to clarify requirements and reduce rework
decisions_made: Career Trajectory UI: longest-duration role is primary (left-aligned); overlapping roles are offset to the right; dynamic blue highlight on timeline on card click
Total YOE calculated as first-job-start to last-job-end span minus breaks >6 months; Industry YOE calculated independently by summing per-industry durations
Replace LinkedIn company data with AI (Grok)-sourced industry classification and company description
Candidate profile header title = title from the primary (longest-duration) role
Implement login/authentication now to support a multi-client SaaS model with shared core data and client-specific summaries
Create initial SaaS test accounts for Celestial and HNC
Establish a pre-sprint wireframe Q&A review process with Lulu and Oshady to clarify requirements before development begins
open_questions: Should the wireframe review session happen before or after sprint planning? The team agreed to hold it but left the timing undecided.
What is the tiebreaker when two overlapping roles have equal duration for determining the primary role?
Which specific GDPR and HIPAA compliance requirements apply to the data architecture, and how will they be addressed?
missing_information: The timing of the wireframe review session relative to sprint planning was explicitly left undecided
Specific AWS account isolation requirements for Metis were not discussed in depth; left to Lulu to investigate
No specific deadlines were set for any of the assigned action items
The standard industry list to be created for company classification was mentioned but no process, source, or owner was assigned

action_items.action: Fix the missing from-to date bug for current roles in the Career Trajectory UI
Implement the new Career Trajectory UI logic: longest-duration role as primary (left-aligned), overlapping roles offset to the right, dynamic blue timeline highlight on card click
Unlink the auto-update between the Total Years of Experience and Industry Experience fields in the UI
Update industry experience rings to start from the 12 o'clock position for visual consistency
Integrate Grok for company data enrichment: scrape only company name from LinkedIn, use Grok to derive industry classification and company description
Implement the new logic for calculating Total Years of Experience (first-start to last-end minus breaks >6 months) and Industry Years of Experience (sum per industry)
Assign the candidate's primary role title (longest-duration role) to the profile header
Create a feature ticket for login/authentication, assigning implementation tasks to Mazar, Nipuna, and Oshady
Schedule the pre-sprint wireframe Q&A review session with Lulu and Oshady before the next sprint
Investigate the current AWS account to confirm Metis is isolated from other Celestial projects

meeting_title: New Hire Onboarding Check-in — Creston & Folke LLP

summary.concise_summary: Three new associates at Creston & Folke LLP (Jordan Tate in Corporate, Reem Saleh in Litigation, Oliver Bassett in Real Estate) met with HR Director Paula Everett and IT Support Specialist Marcus Fong for a first-week temperature check. IT issues included Jordan's DMS Lextrack group permissions not attaching correctly on provisioning (to be fixed immediately after the meeting), a known Westlaw/Chrome browser quirk not yet documented in onboarding materials, and Oliver's distorted email signature logo. Policy questions covered the official 1,900-hour annual billable hours target (versus a 2,000-hour department-level stretch goal mentioned by Reem's supervisor), the $100 pre-approval threshold for expenses (Jordan's $80 lunch was compliant), and the remote work policy (no formal waiting period but a strong informal 90-day in-office norm, especially in Real Estate). Onboarding feedback praised the buddy and mentorship programs but identified the information-dense first-day orientation, IT provisioning delays, and the absence of a building floor map as areas for improvement. Paula committed to following up with all three associates by end of next week.
key_discussion_points: Jordan Tate unable to open DMS Lextrack matter files due to group permissions not attaching correctly during provisioning — a known issue that Marcus can fix with a ten-minute manual correction
Westlaw requires Chrome, not Edge — a known browser quirk not yet documented in onboarding materials
Oliver Bassett's email signature logo was distorted due to incorrect manual resizing; correct template to be provided

Discrepancy between the firm's official 1,900-hour billable hours target (handbook and offer letter) and the 2,000-hour figure mentioned by Reem's Litigation supervisor (likely a department-level stretch goal)

Expense pre-approval threshold is $100 for supervising partner sign-off; Jordan's $80 client lunch does not require pre-approval and can be submitted through Concur within 30 days with the client matter number

Remote work policy: no formal waiting period exists in the written policy, but a strong 90-day in-office norm is standard, especially for Real Estate; associates advised to raise the topic with their supervisor at the three-month mark
Buddy system and mentorship pairing highlighted as the most effective onboarding elements by all three new hires
First-day orientation identified as information overload; suggestion to space it out or send a post-orientation recap email (a previously discussed but unimplemented idea)
IT provisioning delay: Reem did not have full system access until Wednesday, stalling her first two days; root cause is late submission of provisioning requests from HR
No building floor map exists; Oliver discovered the kitchen on day three; suggestion to add a physical and digital version to onboarding
decisions_made: Marcus to fix Jordan's DMS Lextrack group permissions immediately after the meeting
Marcus to send Oliver the correct email signature template after the meeting
Marcus to add a browser preference note (Chrome for Westlaw) to the onboarding checklist
Paula to confirm alignment on billable hours expectation between the firm handbook and Litigation department guidance, and clarify directly with Reem
Paula to review and clarify remote work policy language in the handbook for first-year associates
Paula to explore triggering IT provisioning earlier in the offer process to prevent day-one access delays
Paula to develop a post-orientation recap email summarizing key information from first-day orientation
Paula and Marcus to create a building floor map and publish a digital version on the intranet
open_questions: Does Reem's Litigation supervisor apply a higher 2,000-hour stretch goal, and how should first-year associates in Litigation reconcile department-level expectations with the firm's official 1,900-hour benchmark?

Are there other undocumented IT quirks beyond the Westlaw/Chrome issue that should be captured in the onboarding materials?

action_items.action: Fix Jordan Tate's DMS Lextrack group permissions so she can access Corporate matter files
Send Oliver Bassett the correct email signature template file
Add a note to the onboarding documentation specifying that Westlaw must be opened in Chrome, not Edge
Confirm alignment between the firm's 1,900-hour billable target and the 2,000-hour figure referenced by Reem's Litigation supervisor; clarify the correct benchmark directly with Reem
Review and update handbook language on remote work eligibility for first-year associates to reflect the 90-day in-office norm
Explore moving the IT provisioning request earlier in the offer or hiring process to eliminate day-one and day-two access gaps
Develop and send a post-orientation recap email summarizing the key information covered on day one

meeting_title: Product Roadmap Sprint Kickoff — Moonbeam Labs Mobile Checkout

summary.concise_summary: The Moonbeam Labs mobile checkout team held a 15-minute sprint kickoff and set the sprint goal as shipping a testable mobile checkout flow covering payment, address input, and order confirmation. Three active tickets were sized: MCO-47 (address autocomplete, 8 points) was pulled in from the backlog after Anika identified it as a prerequisite blocker for the order confirmation screen; the Android 3DS redirect flow fix (3 points, carry-over) is expected to complete by end of week with QA testing scheduled for the following Monday; and the order confirmation screen (5 points) is pending a revised UX spec from Ravi by Wednesday. Two blockers were identified: the QA staging environment has no dedicated Stripe test account (Cara has been using her personal account), which Jake will resolve today with the infra team; and the order object schema in Confluence is potentially outdated, which Jake will locate and post before EOD. A new ticket MCO-51 was created for the Stripe staging setup as a 1-point infra task.

Stripe integration on Android is mostly complete; 3DS redirect flow fix is approximately 80% done and expected to finish by end of week

source: Q3 Budget Review — Vortex Analytics Inc.txt
meeting_title: Q3 Budget Review — Vortex Analytics Inc.

summary.concise_summary: The Vortex Analytics finance team reviewed Q3 actuals and approved the provisional Q4 budget. Q3 total spend of $2.41 million exceeded the $2.18 million forecast by approximately 11%, driven by two main variances: cloud infrastructure ($498k actual vs $340k budget) due to an unplanned August data migration (~$80k) and higher-than-scoped data volumes from three new enterprise clients onboarded in September; and contractor costs ($210k vs $175k forecast) due to two contractors extended past their July end date without a formal change request. For Q4, the team approved a provisional budget of $2.35 million with cloud infrastructure raised to $420k including a 10% contingency. Divya will migrate Redshift clusters to reserved instances before October 1st, potentially reducing the cloud line to $390k. Going forward, the team agreed on a 15%-above-forecast cloud spend alert mechanism and a formal change request requirement for all contractor extensions. Lily will add a contractor extension field to the monthly variance report.
key_discussion_points: Q3 total overspend: $2.41M actual vs $2.18M forecast (approximately 11% over budget)

Cloud infrastructure variance: $498k actual vs $340k budget (+$158k); causes: unplanned August data migration (~$80k in compute and egress costs) and unexpectedly high pipeline cluster utilization from three new enterprise clients onboarded in September

Contractor cost variance: $210k actual vs $175k forecast; two contractors extended through end of September (originally scoped to finish in July) to cover a client delivery slip; communicated via email but not as a formal change request
Engineering team monitored cluster utilization but expected it to normalize after onboarding; no escalation to finance before the overspend became material
Q4 cloud infrastructure proposed at $420k, incorporating new client data volumes and a 10% contingency buffer
Migrating Redshift clusters to reserved instances before October expected to reduce cloud unit costs by approximately 12–15%, potentially bringing Q4 cloud spend down to $390k
Q4 contractor budget: $160k (assumes no extensions); Oscar flagged risk of additional resource need if pipeline deals close in Q4
Agreed process change: Divya to set up a cloud console alert when spend exceeds 15% above monthly forecast
Agreed process change: all contractor extensions must be submitted as formal change requests, not informal emails
Lily to add a contractor extension field to the monthly variance report to surface extensions before they become budget overruns
decisions_made: Approve Q4 provisional budget of $2.35 million with cloud infrastructure at $420k (including 10% contingency)
Divya to migrate Redshift clusters to reserved instances before October 1st; if completed, Q4 cloud budget can be trimmed to $390k
Implement a cloud spend alert triggering when spend exceeds 15% above monthly forecast; Divya to configure in the cloud console
All future contractor extensions require a formal change request (not just email notification)
Lily to add a contractor extension tracking field to the monthly variance report
Oscar to flag any likely pipeline close to Tom before committing contractor resource
open_questions: Will Q4 pipeline deals close, requiring additional contractor resource beyond the $160k budget?
Is the 15% alert threshold the right trigger level given historical variance patterns, or does it need calibration?
What exactly were the scoped vs actual data volumes for the three new enterprise clients — were scope estimates materially off, and does this affect future client onboarding projections?
missing_information: The $80k August data migration cost was an estimate; exact figures were not reviewed or confirmed during the meeting
No specific target date was confirmed for the reserved instances migration beyond 'before October' (i.e., before October 1st)
The formal change request process for contractor extensions was agreed to but no owner was assigned to document or enforce it going forward

action_items.action: Set up cloud spend budget alerts in the cloud console to trigger when monthly spend exceeds 15% above forecast
Migrate Redshift clusters to reserved instances to reduce cloud unit costs by 12–15%; complete before October 1st
Send the updated Q4 budget sheet incorporating the provisional allocations discussed to the full group
Add a contractor extension field to the monthly variance report so extensions are visible before becoming budget overruns
Flag any pipeline deal that looks likely to close in Q4 to Tom before committing to additional contractor resource

flagged_issues: Contractor extension was communicated via email rather than a formal change request — the process gap was acknowledged but no owner was assigned to document or enforce the new formal requirement going forward

Cloud overspend was being monitored at the engineering level but was not escalated to finance before it became a material variance; the new 15% alert addresses this prospectively but the Q3 miss was avoidable

The Redshift reserved instances migration has no firmer deadline than 'before October 1st' — if delayed, the Q4 $420k cloud estimate may need revision and Tom's updated budget sheet may need to be corrected

meeting_title: Campaign Performance Debrief — Tangent Creative Co.

summary.concise_summary: The Tangent Creative Co. marketing team debriefed on the summer launch campaign and aligned on August A/B test priorities. The campaign achieved a 2.4% CTR (above the 2.1% benchmark) but underdelivered on conversion at 1.8% against a 2.5% target, indicating strong click volume but funnel drop-off. Three specific failure points were identified: the animated banner set (0.9% CTR, attributed to insufficient mobile QA before launch), vague CTAs in email messages 4 and 5 causing post-message-3 click-through drop-off, and an unresolved tablet landing page layout bug producing a 68% bounce rate. The best-performing assets were static lifestyle photography ads (3.1% CTR, 2.3% conversion) and an urgency-driven email subject line variant (38% open rate). For August, the team agreed to run three A/B tests: static vs animated creative, landing page headline, and short-form vs long-form funnel flows. Hannah will deliver copy variants by July 12, Priya needs assets by July 15 to set up tracking for a late-July launch, and Sofia will schedule a readiness sync for July 16. Ethan will own the UTM template and investigate the tablet bug today.
key_discussion_points: Summer campaign overall CTR: 2.4% vs 2.1% benchmark (above target); conversion rate: 1.8% vs 2.5% target (below target) — clicks were driven but drop-off occurred in the funnel
Animated banner set: worst performer at 0.9% CTR; Ethan acknowledged mobile QA was insufficient before launch

Email sequence: solid overall open rates (~34%) but click-through dropped sharply after message three; CTAs in messages 4 and 5 were too vague ('learn more'); funnel data confirms drop-off at this exact point
Tablet landing page: 68% bounce rate at the tablet breakpoint; dev team flagged a layout issue two weeks before launch but Ethan believed it was resolved — confirmed it was not
Best-performing assets: static lifestyle photography ads (3.1% CTR, 2.3% conversion rate for that segment)
Best-performing email variant: urgency-driven 'limited time' subject line at 38% open rate — outperformed standard variants despite initial hesitation about the tone

August A/B test priorities: (1) static vs animated creative — resolve the format question with real data; (2) landing page headline refresh (unchanged since March); (3) short-form vs long-form funnel flow comparison
UTM parameter inconsistency in the previous campaign broke half the tracking; Ethan acknowledged ownership and will own the UTM template going forward
All creative assets and copy variants needed by July 15 for a late-July launch; copy due July 12 to allow buffer
decisions_made: Run three A/B tests in August: static vs animated creative, landing page headline, and short-form vs long-form funnel flow
Ethan to own and distribute the UTM parameter template for the August campaign before any assets go live
Hannah to deliver copy variants for A/B tests by July 12, focusing on direct benefit-led messaging vs current approach
Hannah to also revise email messages 4 and 5 CTAs to be more specific (separate from A/B tests — fix regardless)
Priya to set up the tracking structure for all three A/B tests; needs assets and copy by July 15
Sofia to schedule a readiness sync on July 16 to confirm all assets are ready before launch
open_questions: Has the tablet landing page layout bug actually been resolved? Ethan to confirm status by EOD.
What specific benefit-led copy angles will Hannah test in the A/B variants versus the current messaging approach?
What is the target launch date within 'end of July' for the August A/B campaign?
missing_information: The exact launch date was described as 'end of July' but not confirmed as a specific date

source: Vendor Contract Renewal — Nexora Supply Chain.txt
meeting_title: Vendor Contract Renewal — Nexora Supply Chain / Eastbridge Logistics

summary.concise_summary: Nexora Supply Chain's procurement team met with Karl Jensen from Eastbridge Logistics to negotiate renewal terms ahead of the July 5th contract expiry. On volume discounts, the parties agreed to a 6% discount at a 700-shipment-per-month floor with a 60-day notice clause if volumes drop below that level; the proposed 8% tier above 900 shipments remains unresolved, pending Karl's internal sign-off and a formal volume commitment from Nexora. On SLAs, a hard 96% on-time delivery target was agreed with a force majeure carve-out and a new 48-hour invocation notice window replacing the existing vague 'reasonable notice' language. Expanding real-time GPS tracking to Class B freight (not just Class A) was agreed in principle, with Karl to provide per-shipment cost impact by Friday. The penalty structure was revised from 1.5% per event with a 5% cap to 2.5% per event with a 7% cap, plus a new $500 administrative fee for SLA breach reports submitted more than 5 business days after Eastbridge becomes aware. Irene Castellano will produce a revised contract draft within two business days of receiving the Class B tracking cost figures, targeting full execution before June 30th.
key_discussion_points: Current volume discount: flat 4% above 500 shipments/month; Nexora proposed a tiered structure (4% at 500, 6.5% at 700, 8% above 900); Karl countered with 6% at 700 and flagged 8% as requiring both internal approval and a volume commitment from Nexora

Nexora confident in sustaining 700 shipments/month floor; David considers 750 likely but would not commit in writing; January/February historically dip (~620 in prior year) though route consolidation in March may have improved this
60-day notice clause: required by Karl if Nexora volumes drop below the 700 floor, to protect Eastbridge capacity planning
8% tier at 900 shipments: unresolved — requires Karl's internal sign-off and a formal written volume commitment from Nexora
OTD SLA: current contractual target is 94%; three months last year came in at 91–92%, causing downstream warehouse scheduling issues; Nexora requesting a hard 96% target
Karl accepted 96% as a hard contractual target, attributing last year's dips partly to industry-wide port delays
Force majeure: already in the existing contract; carried forward with a new 48-hour invocation notice window (previously 'reasonable notice,' which was ambiguous)
Class B freight GPS tracking: expanding real-time 30-minute updates from Class A only to include Class B is agreed in principle; Karl to confirm per-shipment cost impact by Friday
David flagged that if the Class B tracking cost is more than a few cents per shipment, it changes the economics of the discount tiers
Penalty revision: 1.5% per event / 5% monthly cap → 2.5% per event / 7% cap (Nexora proposed 10%, Karl countered 7%, Fatima accepted)
New $500 administrative fee for late SLA breach reporting: clock starts from when Eastbridge becomes aware of the breach (not the date of occurrence)
decisions_made: 6% volume discount at a 700-shipment-per-month floor with a 60-day notice clause for volume shortfalls
8% discount tier at 900 shipments remains open; outstanding pending Karl's internal approval and a formal volume commitment from Nexora
Hard 96% on-time delivery SLA target with force majeure carve-out
Force majeure invocation notice window: 48 hours from when Eastbridge becomes aware of the triggering event
Expand real-time GPS tracking to Class B freight in principle; final agreement contingent on Karl's cost impact figures (due Friday)
Penalty structure: 2.5% per breach event, 7% monthly invoice cap
New $500 administrative fee for SLA breach reporting delays beyond 5 business days of Eastbridge's awareness date
Irene to draft the revised contract within two business days of receiving Karl's Class B tracking cost figures
Target: all terms agreed and contract ready for signature before June 30th
open_questions: What is the per-shipment cost increase for expanding GPS tracking to Class B freight? (Karl to provide by Friday morning)
Does the Class B tracking cost materially affect the economics of the volume discount tiers?
What formal volume commitment can Nexora offer to unlock the 8% discount tier above 900 shipments?
missing_information: Karl did not have the Class B freight tracking cost figures available during the meeting — exact cost unknown until Friday
No formal volume commitment level from Nexora for the 8% discount tier was proposed or discussed

The Q1 seasonal volume dip risk (January/February averaging ~620 in prior year) was raised by David but not proposed as a formal contract carve-out, despite Nexora potentially triggering the 60-day notice clause during those months

action_items.action: Provide the per-shipment cost impact of expanding real-time GPS tracking to Class B freight
Review the Class B tracking cost figures from Karl and confirm whether the cost is acceptable or requires renegotiation of the discount tiers

Draft revised contract incorporating all agreed terms: 6%/700 discount with 60-day notice clause, 96% OTD SLA with 48-hour force majeure notice, 2.5%/7% penalty structure, $500 late-reporting fee, and Class B tracking clause once cost is confirmed
action_items.due_date: Within 2 business days of receiving Karl's Class B cost figures

action_items.action: Seek internal sign-off on the 8% discount tier at 900 shipments and report position back to Greg
Confirm whether Nexora can provide a formal volume commitment to unlock the 8% discount tier, incorporating demand planning data

flagged_issues: The 8% discount tier at 900 shipments was left entirely unresolved — it requires both Karl's internal sign-off and a formal volume commitment from Nexora; if either is delayed, the full discount structure cannot be finalized before the June 30th deadline
Karl did not bring the Class B freight tracking cost figures to the meeting — this is an unknown that could affect the contract economics and delay Irene's draft if not received by Friday

Q1 seasonal volume risk: David acknowledged January/February volumes historically dip to ~620 shipments, which could trigger the 60-day notice clause; no Q1 carve-out was proposed in the contract language despite the discussion
